result:
I keep thinking about the way light pools on my kitchen floor at 4:17 p.m.—not 4:15, not 4:20, but that exact, stubborn minute when the sun slips between the two oaks out back and the cheap window glass refracts everything into a spilled-coin shimmer. It’s the same color as the penny-candy varnish on the nails of the woman who ran the corner store when I was seven, the one that smelled of malted milk balls and the ghost of her Tabu perfume. She wore bubble-gum pink every Friday and let me buy a single Atomic Fireball even though my mother had forbidden them. I’d hold it like a tiny red planet, daring myself to let it scald my tongue until the outer shell cracked and the cinnamon nova went super-critical. That burn proved I could withstand things.

Now I withstand different things: calendar pings, the wet click of the neighbor’s sprinkler, the way my father’s voice still attaches itself to certain syllables of my own when I’m tired. But I withstand them in the same body that once hid behind cereal boxes to finish library books before dinner, knees printed with carpet diamonds, heart revving at the thought of unfinished chapters. The body is a traitor and a confidant; it keeps the score of every sunburn, every sneaked candy, every promise to leave town by nineteen, and still it offers up these daily miracles of light on linoleum like hush-money.

Sometimes I rake the shards of that light together with my bare feet, pretending I can collect enough to fill a mason jar, a luminous archive I could open at 3 a.m. when the newsfeed is a slaughterhouse and the ceiling rehearses the same cracked spine of plaster. I’d unscrew the lid and let the 4:17 p.m. kitchen pour out—copper, sugar, the creak of the old freezer, the dog barking two houses down—everything exactly as it was, uncurated, unrepentant. Maybe the woman with the bubble-gum nails would be there too, arms crossed, telling me you can’t eat more than one Fireball or you’ll blister your soul. I’d listen this time, or not; either way she’d laugh, and the laugh would smell like malt and summer and the moment before choice.

I think we’re all hoarding some version of that jar. We walk around with pockets full of obsolete light, trading it in secret: a bus driver’s wristwatch flash, the emerald seizure of sunlight on a windshield that makes you miss a turn, the rectangle of window-glow on your bedroom wall that looks exactly like the map of a country that no longer exists. We think we’re paying attention, but mostly we’re just cupping these seconds like rainwater in shaking hands, sipping when we’re desperate, forgetting the taste by the next drought.

And still the day arrives when you realize the floor will keep shining whether you witness it or not, that the oaks will reposition themselves inch by inch toward their own slow applause, that candy burns and carpet diamonds and even fathers’ voices have half-lives. The realization should feel like freedom, but instead it feels like walking into an empty classroom and seeing the chalk still spinning in mid-air after the bell. You can’t decide whether to finish the equation or erase it. So you do neither—you pocket the chalk, step into the hallway, and start writing on whatever surface will hold the dust: a shopping list, a love letter, a warning, a map. The light follows, patient, unacknowledged, like a dog that’s learned you will eventually drop something.

I write this now because the jar is open, the light is leaking, and the woman with the pink nails is somewhere—old, maybe, or nowhere—her Friday polish chipped into constellations. I write because the floor is beginning to dim, the oaks shifting their alliance, the sun already negotiating tomorrow’s ransom. I write because the Fireball is down to a seed of dye on my tongue, and the burn is almost—almost—gone.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
